speaker: interviewer
text: August 2nd, Los Angeles, we're interviewing Cathy Weiss. Cathy, so you arrived, you were on the train, and you arrived June 3rd, 1944, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: June 3rd, 1944, in the morning, we arrived. We didn't know where we arrived. We arrived somewhere. The doors were opened very fast, and from every direction, very strong reflectors-- lights-- in our eyes. In front of us, it was a platform, of course, full with SS soldiers-- many, many of them. And we heard already the yelling, fast, fast, out of the cattle cars. That's what we heard from then on all the time. Fast, fast. That was the motto. Well, fast we got out from the cattle cars, but not everyone was able to get out. Some people, very few, but some, who were either too old, or too young, or too sick, they did not survive this trip. Very few, but some.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very hard on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard on the train, yes. So we got out. We had that little bag with us-- with the food, one change of clothes. We heard the yelling, put it down, you will get it later, all right. Then, again, men on one side, women on the other side, women and children on the other side. That was the last time that I saw my father. My mother, grandmother, myself, all the other women and children on one side. We were so confused. Really didn't know where we are and what's happening. And it was such a shock when they told us the men on one side. And we just hardly had the time enough just to kiss goodbye my father. Well, we were confused.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you kiss goodbye?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a sense that you were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they just said separated. Men on one side, women on the other side, so we knew that we have to go in opposite direction. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say anything to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. We were sure that we shall see each other soon, but we didn't know when. Of course, and as we were so confused, we didn't know what to do. There was a group of prisoners of higher ranks who were ordered, just there, to try to pacify us, to make a little order among us, because we really were so confused. And I remember they just told that young mothers should give the children to the grandmothers. It was no explanation why. So many young women didn't want to part from the kids and give it to the grandmothers, but some did. Of course, later on, we knew perfectly well why-- because the little children and the older people were killed right away. But we really didn't know why we were told. So they wanted to help. That's what it was. Again, yelling, form lines of five. There were thousands and thousands of lines of five. Our line of five was grandmother, my mother, myself, my girlfriend Eva, her mother Helen. That's how was our line of five. Then we had to go through a big gate. Above the big gate, it was the inscription saying work makes you free, which was the most cynical lie what one could ever imagine. Because, as we found out, we arrived to Auschwitz, and Auschwitz was no working camp. Auschwitz was extermination camp. Auschwitz was the biggest factory of death what one could ever imagine. In the front of the gate was standing a very proper looking SS officer by the name of Dr. Josef Mengele, known also as Angel of Death, the famous killer of Auschwitz. He was just standing there, holding a stick in one hand. And the lines of five had to pass in front of him through the gate. He was cutting into lines of five, sending some people to the left, some people to the right. He was cutting into our line of five also, sending my mother and my grandma to the left. My mother, at that time, was 41 years old. We were never separated before. I was not 18 yet. I had my 18th birthday, so-called 18th birthday, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So mother, grandmother sent to the left. Myself, Eva, her mother to the right. Mother and I, we just couldn't get apart, no way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, some soldiers separated us. They had to go to the left. I had to go to the right with Eva and her mother. I still remember my mother's eyes as she looked after me. That I shall never forget. We continued to the right. It was such a muddy road. Even if it was June, it was a cold day there and muddy. And we entered toward going into Auschwitz. That was the main road. All around us, like all the time from then on, nonstop, heavily armed SS soldiers. We go on the main road, we look to the right, we look to the left. All over we have seen electric barbed wire fences. Behind those, we saw some women like they would have come from another planet. They were like skeletons. They were bald. They wore some gray or gray with white stripes, so-called dresses, I should say rags. They were the prisoners of Auschwitz. In very short time, we looked exactly the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were ordered into a very big barrack full of soldiers, like all the time. Again, yelling, undress very fast. Well, we just looked at each other. How can you undress in front of so many strangers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, it was no time to ask there any questions. In no time, we were undressed. In no time, our hair was shaved, and we were standing there nude, bald, terrified. So confused that I cannot even describe it to you. Of course, as we later on, we found out, they use up the hair in industry. And, of course, they used up our clothes, too. Well, then, we were ordered into another big barrack for shower. We entered. We see many, many shower heads on the ceiling. And, of course, at that time, we didn't know-- how could we-- that the shower heads doesn't mean necessarily that it has to be water. It could be just as well the famous Zyklon gas, what they use in Auschwitz for killing. At that time, it was water, some cold water. Of course, there was no soap, or towel, or anything like that. Some cold, little cold water. Then, out from that, yelling, get out, into another big barrack, where we had to wait for quite a while, nude, wet, cold, until we got the same kind of rags-- so-called clothes-- what we have seen on the other prisoner women as we entered the main road in Auschwitz. As we know, Auschwitz was composed of many camps. Our group at that time was taken into camp C. There was camp A, camp B, camp C, and so on. Camp C, we enter camp C, all around us, like always the heavily armed SS soldiers. We go in and we see many, many barracks. Many rows of barracks, they all look alike. I was taken, with many others, of course, into barrack C. We enter barrack C. The barrack commander, a young Jewish, Polish prisoner woman who was there already for quite a while, she was already expecting us. We came in, we look around, we saw many, many rows of three-layered bunk beds. That's where we were sleeping. Well, that was in barrack eight and many other barracks, but in between-- I just mention it also-- that many other barracks like 22, 24, 28, and many others, they did not have the three-layered bunk bed. The other girls were sleeping on the floor, on the cement. We were sleeping in the bunk beds. Well, later, to that sleeping facilities, we come in, we look around. There was even a small window. Through the small window, we looked through. We saw flame, we saw smoke. I remember one of the girls asking the barrack commander, what's the flame, what's the smoke. And she answered that those are your parents, your children, your friends, your family. Everyone who was sent to the left was taken into gas chamber, then into crematoriums and burned. And that's the smoke, and that's the flame. Well, of course, we didn't want to believe her. That seemed quite impossible, even if it was some unusual smell in the air, which, of course, later on, we found out that's the smell of the burning flesh. We didn't believe her. I remember just a few weeks earlier my father saying that we live in the 20th century and really nothing drastic could happen. But my father was wrong because that's what was happening in Auschwitz. Day and night, nonstop, the crematoriums were working. The sky was always covered with flame, with smoke. And we always smelled that unusual smell in the air, the smell of the burning flesh, which we got used to it already. We didn't even notice it later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cathy, when this woman explained to you that the smoke was your parents, your families, the transport you came with, did you-- do you remember the feeling that you had? Was she telling you this to be helpful or was she telling you to be cruel, to be sadistic? What was her motivation for telling you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my mind it was cruelty because it couldn't be true. It couldn't happen. As I remembered, my father said we live in the 20th century, so how could that happen? No. That was unthinkable. No, I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But looking back at her facial expression, how she said it to you, did she say it to save your life? Did she say it to warn you, to orient you, to inform you? Or did she say it to be cruel to you? Because it was true, what she told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I think back, no, she was not cruel. She just told the truth. One of us asked and she told us. And maybe she thought that it's better if we know in time, maybe. At that time, I couldn't believe that she was nice. I thought it was cruelty at that time because I didn't believe it. Oh, but very soon, very, very soon, we found out that that's true because that was going on all the time, day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How soon did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in a few days. In a few days, when we saw always the smoke, always the flame, and the smell. So we found out, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you thought, what you said, what you felt? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was desperate. I was shocked, I was desperate. I just didn't know how to express my feelings in any way. When I think about the time when we arrived to Auschwitz, I call it the time when Cathy Lebi died. That's-- in my mind, that's what it is, because that's when I died-- when we arrived to Auschwitz, and when I was separated from my father and from my mother, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that still true?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's true, because, after that, I was not and I am not the same person what would I have been if that wouldn't have happened, no. I consider it-- that-- that part of my life was finished. That was another life after that. I absolutely believe in that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, maybe tell you how we lived in Auschwitz. We were sleeping on those three-layered bunk beds. Each bunk bed was very, very comfortable, actually, almost like a king size bed or a queen size bed. So it would have been wonderful for two people, but we were 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six in one direction this way, six in the other direction, head to toe. And, of course, on our sides, like sardines in a box, because otherwise there was no room. If one of us had to turn, then everyone had to turn in unison because it just was no way otherwise. Of course, we were sleeping on the wood. Of course, there were no linen, or blankets, or pillows, or anything like that. In the morning, we had to get up very early. Of course, there was no watch or clock to look at it, but we sort of figured out that it had to be approximately between 4:00 and 5:00, something like that. Again, yelling, get out, roll call, form lines of five. There were thousands and hundred thousands of formed lines of five. We had to stand roll call for many, many hours. We were counted, recounted from the smallest official finally to the highest official, whose name was Irma Grese, SS woman commander of Auschwitz, a very good-looking young SS officer woman. And she came most of the time with two German Shepherds. The dogs were trained to kill. She was unbelievably mean. After roll call, still having the lines of five, we got breakfast. Breakfast was always the same thing, some kind of a black coffee. More like black water, I would say. Each line of five, we had one cup. And each five of us, we were drinking one after the other, counting the sips to make sure that everyone has approximately about the same amount of coffee-- so-called coffee. After then, we got the daily bread portion, which was like approximately two slices of bread would be today, but more like sawdust. Very seldom, occasionally we got some little rotten cheese. Then we didn't do anything because, as I told you, Auschwitz was no working camp. It was extermination camp. So we were just sitting on the ground talking to each other, sometimes not even talking. And the conversation was about family, about freedom, about food, about variety of foods because we were so very, very hungry. In the afternoon, again, the same roll call for many, many, hours. Again, counted, recounted, from the smallest official until Irma Grese had counted us, and then we got dinner. Dinner was a very thin soup. Each line of five, we had one bowl. Of course, there was no spoon or anything like that. And we were drinking, each five of us from the same bowl, counting again the sips to make sure that everyone has approximately about the same amount of soup. Then we didn't do anything again. Other thing in Auschwitz, the humiliation. We were reminded with words, with facts, all the time, that we are not human beings. We are much less than a bug and what you can step in a second. Well, what-- I have the cleanest memories still in my mind of the humiliations, which I am going to tell you. It is the outhouse. Outhouse was a very big barrack. There were many, many seats next to each other, and there were very many rows of seats. So we were sitting there, many, many of us, while, most of the time, SS soldiers were running back and forth there between the rows, occasionally hitting us with the sticks, what they had in their hand, and yelling, the motto what we heard all the time, fast, fast. Then we went back to the barrack, but that was such a humiliating feeling. Such a shame, I still remember. Other thing in Auschwitz, the selection. The selection went on all the time, and it was always the same person who did it, Dr. Josef Mengele, the Angel of Death. It happened most of the time, 99% of the time, during the night. Still, I don't know why. Maybe because we were more confused or what, not that it would have mattered that we are confused or not. I still don't know why. It happened during the night. It was him who came in. He was just standing there, most of the time, putting one hand in one pocket, the other one holding that stick-- what we called among us the orchestra conductor's baton, because it reminded us of that. He was even humming some tunes most of the time. We had to walk in front of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the tunes were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some waltzes many times, yes. We had to walk in front of him, almost always, 99% of the time, nude. And as we were walking in front of him, he was just pointing to us, pointing to the left, pointing to the right. Of course, much, much, much more often to the left than to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just pointing to the left, pointing to the right. And, of course, at that time, we knew perfectly well that left meant crematorium, gas chamber, right meant life, maybe for a day, maybe for a week. Who knows? For that-- for that minute, at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the criteria? It's very hard to tell you. I don't know, because we all look like skeletons after quite a short time. But those of us who looked more skeleton-like than the other one, anyone who had the rash, or a pimple, or anything on the body, that was definitely pointed to the left to gas chamber, to crematorium. Who was pointed to the right, then in the barrack, still in the barrack. Other thing in Auschwitz-- occasionally, they took us to showers. I was going to ask you something about Mengele. If you remember, did you ever get close enough to see his face?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember seeing in his face, in his eyes?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very proper looking always. There was no feeling in it at all. Just like, I guess, like a shop clerk doing the job putting the merchandise here, putting the merchandise there, nothing. Just looking at the merchandise, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when they found the remains of his body? Do you remember how you felt about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe someone can get you some water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, maybe someone can bring me the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember thinking when you found out that Mengele was dead, and that we knew that it was his remains?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I found out that he was dead, I was really upset that he could live such a long, good life. Sure, I was happy that he was dead, but I was not happy the way he died and when he died. That was one of my dreams, daydreams, all my life, I should say before that. Maybe, once, they will find Mengele, and maybe I can be there when we beat him to death, or when he will be executed, and maybe I can put also a bullet somehow in him. Yes, that was the feeling all the time. And when he was found, sure it was good that he was dead, but I was upset that he was not killed before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that he was conducting medical experiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- while we were in the camp, no. In the camp, we did not know anything about anybody, about anything, nothing, zero.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your hatred of him, what you described now-- is that something that you felt instinctively because of your contact with him, or because you learned so much about him after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. What I learned about him after the war, that was the main thing. Of course, I knew in the camp also, I hated him tremendously, because I knew that who he was pointing anyone to the left, that was taken to the gas chamber, to the crematorium. I hated them tremendously for that. I hated them tremendously because he sent my mother, my grandmother, and all those children, all those people to the left, to the gas chamber. The experiments, I found out only after the war and, of course, I hated him for that also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe it? Could you put it together? Could you put the man together with his deeds? Was it hard to put him together, this gentleman, together with the deeds that he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because he was always very proper looking. He didn't show any anger, any hatred, no. Just indifference on the face. Someone who does a pleasant job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you would have recognized him if you had seen him again?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I would have seen him maybe in 10 or 20 years after, probably yes. Later, probably not, because people change. I am sure he had went through some operations, too. I am sure. And I change with age, too. So everyone is changing. No, I don't think so that after, let's say, 20 years, I would have recognized him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have a number tattooed on your arm. I know that many of the transports didn't get them. Did your whole transport not have them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is the explanation? We arrived-- we arrived June the 3rd. It was a big transport. The transports were coming from all over Hungary. That was for the Hungarian Jews' killing time. And they were so busy with the gassing, with the selection, with all that, so they did not have time to tattoo us. And that's why we don't have the tattoo, as I found out. No other reason. That's the reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I also wanted to ask you, could you use the bathrooms, if you can call them bathrooms. Could you use them when you wanted to? Or were there only specific times when you could go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we couldn't use it when we wanted to. Only specific times when to go. That was so humiliating.

speaker: interviewee
text: So where we were, that they took us occasionally to showers. And, of course, we never knew for sure that it's going to be water or it's going to be gas. We are going back to the selections. The selections went on all the time and long, long, long time. I went through 11 selections. And I-- still, I don't know how I was able to count them, but I did. And I remember there were 11 selections. So what do you think helped you to pass the selections? What do you think--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason?

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, 11 selections, that's a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the reason, I haven't the slightest idea. I guess that I didn't have any pimples, any red spots on my body. And I was also skeleton, but maybe-- I consider it just luck, because I don't know any other explanation for anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because there was no logic in anything that happened in the camps. There was no prediction. Nobody knew anything-- what's going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was just happening like that. It was fate, or luck, or whatever. I don't know how should I call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you unusually energetic in comparison to other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very pretty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all were sort of pretty at that age, I guess. But nobody was really pretty in the camp, because we all looked like skeletons, and we all were bald, and we were all dirty. And we looked terrible. I believe that what-- actually, they did not consider us humans. I guess we didn't even look human at that time, already. They all looked terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to pause to change tapes. We'll be right back.
